file_name: Jatmiko and Pristyanto - 2023 - Investigating the effectiveness of various convolutional neural network model architectures for skin.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to classify melanoma skin cancer. It trained and compared four CNN architectures (VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, and MobileNet) on a dataset of 9605 skin images to obtain classification results including accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors built and compared four CNN architectures (VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, and MobileNet) on a specific dataset of 9605 skin images. It includes a 'Research Method' section detailing data acquisition, preprocessing, and model training, followed by 'Result and Analysis' sections reporting specific performance metrics (accuracy, loss, precision, recall, F1-score) for each model, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Investigating The Effectiveness of Various Convolutional Neural Network Model Architectures for Skin Cancer Melanoma Classification
journal: Matrik: Jurnal Manajemen, Teknik Informatika, dan Rekayasa Komputer
brief_summary: This study investigated the effectiveness of four Convolutional Neural Network architectures—VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, and MobileNet—for classifying melanoma skin cancer. The researchers utilized a public Kaggle dataset containing 9,605 skin images divided into benign and malignant classes, which were augmented and split into 75% training and 25% testing data. The models were trained using the Adam optimizer with sparse categorical cross-entropy loss over 50 epochs with a batch size of 32. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices. The Xception model achieved the highest performance with 93% accuracy, 19% loss, 93% precision, 93.5% recall, and a 93% F1-score. Consequently, the study concluded that Xception provides the most stable and accurate performance for melanoma classification among the tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the Kaggle dataset (4605 images) used for binary classification of skin cancer. The study specifically aims to classify melanoma skin cancer.
The other class in the Kaggle dataset (5000 images) used for binary classification. The paper groups these as 'benign' to contrast with malignant melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer into benign and malignant classes
all_task_types.details: The paper investigates the effectiveness of CNN architectures (VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, MobileNet) for classifying melanoma skin cancer. The dataset consists of two classes: benign (5000 images) and malignant (4605 images). The models use sparse categorical cross-entropy loss and sigmoid activation for binary classification. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle containing 9605 skin images. The dataset was divided into benign and malignant classes. The data underwent preprocessing including resizing to 128x128 pixels, data augmentation (resizing, rotating between 0-20 degrees, and horizontal flipping), and rescaling pixel values from 0-255 to 0-1.

use.training: Used for training the VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, and MobileNet models. Data was augmented during each epoch.
use.testing: Used for testing the models to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, loss, precision, recall, F1-score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive metrics (accuracy, loss, precision, recall, F1-score) derived from confusion matrices without statistical significance analysis.

Xception.notes: Highest accuracy and lowest loss among tested models. Precision: Benign 94%, Malignant 92%. Recall: Benign 93%, Malignant 94%. F1-Score: Benign 93%, Malignant 93%.

VGG-16.notes: Precision: Benign 86%, Malignant 96%. Recall: Benign 96%, Malignant 84%. F1-Score: Benign 91%, Malignant 89%.

MobileNet.notes: Precision: Benign 93%, Malignant 88%. Recall: Benign 89%, Malignant 93%. F1-Score: Benign 91%, Malignant 91%.

LeNet.notes: Precision: Benign 89%, Malignant 90%. Recall: Benign 91%, Malignant 88%. F1-Score: Benign 90%, Malignant 89%.

architecture_used.main_model: Four CNN architectures: VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, and MobileNet
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (models were trained from scratch or details not specified in context)

VGG-16.description: Five building blocks with convolutional and max pooling layers. Block 1-2: 2 conv layers + 1 max pooling. Block 3-4: 3 conv layers + 1 max pooling. Block 5: 3 conv layers + 1 max pooling. Filters: 64, 128, 256, 512, 512.

LeNet.description: Standard LeNet architecture used for comparison.

Xception.description: Uses separable convolutional layers, batch normalization, and add layers. Includes entry flow, middle flow (repeated 8 times), and exit flow.
layers: Entry Flow (Convolutional, Separable Convolutional, Batch Normalization, Max Pooling)
Middle Flow (Activation, Separable Convolutional, Batch Normalization, Add layers)
Exit Flow (Activation, Separable Convolutional, Batch Normalization, Max Pooling)

preprocessing_techniques: Data Resizing: Images were resized to a standardized size of 128x128 pixels.
Data Rescaling: Pixel values were normalized from the range 0-255 to 0-1.
Data Augmentation (Rotation): Images were rotated randomly between 0 and 20 degrees.
Data Augmentation (Horizontal Flip): Images were flipped horizontally to increase data variation.
Data Splitting: The dataset was divided into 75% training data and 25% testing data.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were resized to a standardized size of 128x128 pixels.
Images were rotated randomly between 0 and 20 degrees.
Images were flipped horizontally to increase data variation.

conclusions: The Xception model demonstrated the best performance among the tested architectures, achieving 93% accuracy, 19% loss, 93% precision, 93.5% recall, and a 93% F1-score.
The VGG-16 model achieved 90% accuracy and 27% loss, with an average precision of 91%, recall of 90%, and F1-score of 90%.
The MobileNet model achieved 90.8% accuracy and 22.5% loss, with an average precision of 90.5%, recall of 91%, and F1-score of 91%.
The LeNet model achieved the lowest performance among the tested models with 89.7% accuracy and 28% loss, with an average precision, recall, and F1-score of 89.5%.

The study concludes that the Xception architecture provides the most stable and accurate performance for classifying melanoma skin cancer compared to VGG-16, LeNet, and MobileNet when using the same dataset and preprocessing process.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public Kaggle dataset (9,605 images) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance with 5,000 benign images versus 4,605 malignant images; the authors explicitly note this as a limitation and suggest adding more malignant data in future work.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; model comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, loss, precision, recall, F1-score).

Data leakage risk exists as data augmentation (rotation, flipping) was applied during each epoch rather than being fixed prior to splitting, potentially allowing information from the test set to influence training dynamics if not strictly isolated.

The study does not report whether pre-trained weights were used for the CNN architectures (VGG-16, LeNet, Xception, MobileNet), making reproducibility of the specific performance levels difficult to verify.

Clinical relevance is limited as the models were trained on a general skin cancer dataset rather than specifically on dermoscopic images or a curated melanoma-only cohort, and no comparison against dermatologist performance was provided.
The input image resolution was reduced to 128x128 pixels, which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for melanoma detection compared to higher-resolution clinical standards.
